narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.30 and an LSAT score of 35, you've shown some promising academic strengths in your profile, along with a high family income bracket that places you above average in socioeconomic background. You're planning to attend law school full-time, which is the typical path for most students at 92.7% in the dataset. However, the admissions model, trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated a 44% probability that you would fail the bar exam—high enough to trigger a rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates likely to pass on their first try. The most influential factor pushing the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the average of about 5.5, signaling to the model that your later undergraduate performance placed you in the lower end compared to peers who typically succeed on the bar. This stood out as the top driver, outweighing other elements because it strongly indicated potential challenges in sustaining high-level academic performance through law school and the exam. Your undergraduate GPA actually worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success since your 3.30 exceeds the typical 3.21 average and suggests stronger foundational skills than many applicants. Similarly, your LSAT score of 35 nudged things positively, as it's close to the dataset average of 36.7 and reflects solid reasoning ability aligned with bar passers. Overall, while your profile beats averages in some key areas like GPA, the model saw your lower Year 3 decile as a red flag that overshadowed these positives, especially when compared to students who passed the bar, who generally ranked higher across all grade deciles. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a high-income background—many in similar demographics succeed—but the data patterns the model learned highlighted this specific gap in your record. To move forward, consider retaking courses or pursuing a post-baccalaureate program to boost your later-grade deciles, while leveraging your GPA and LSAT strengths in appeals or reapplications to other schools. You might also seek targeted bar prep resources early to demonstrate readiness. With focused improvements, you can shift future predictions in your favor and pursue your legal ambitions.
